stem: On a motorway with three lanes in your direction and light traffic, which lane should you normally use?

choices.text: The left lane, so you can react to slower traffic
The middle lane, as a compromise
The right lane, returning to it after overtaking
Whichever lane has the smoothest surface

explanation.short: You must keep right; the left and middle lanes are for overtaking.
explanation.examTip: Sitting in the middle lane when the right is free is a classic test failure.

stem: Inside a built-up area with two lanes in your direction, you may:

choices.text: Use either lane as suits your route, but not weave between them
Only use the right lane at all times
Drive in the left lane to overtake parked cars on the right
Use the left lane whenever traffic is heavy

explanation.short: Inside built-up areas you may choose the lane that suits your route, but you must not weave.
explanation.examTip: The keep-right duty is stricter outside built-up areas and on motorways.

stem: On which side do you normally overtake another vehicle?

choices.text: The right
The left
Whichever side has more space
Only on a wide shoulder

explanation.short: Overtaking is done on the left.
explanation.examTip: An exception is a vehicle signalling and turning left — pass it on the right.

stem: Where is overtaking always forbidden?

choices.text: On a straight road inside a built-up area
Just before or on a pedestrian crossing
On a road with a broken centre line
When traffic moves at the speed limit

explanation.short: You may not overtake on or just before a pedestrian crossing.
explanation.examTip: The same applies to railway crossings and blind crests.

stem: Another driver is overtaking you on a country road. What should you do?

choices.text: Accelerate so they cannot pass
Keep your line, easing off if needed to help them complete the move
Move into the oncoming lane to let them by on the right
Flash your headlights repeatedly

explanation.short: Do not accelerate when being overtaken; ease off if needed to let them finish safely.
explanation.examTip: Speeding up while being overtaken is dangerous and a serious test fault.

stem: You see a red cycle suggestion lane (broken line) along the carriageway. As a car driver you:

choices.text: Must never drive on it under any circumstance
May drive on it when necessary, giving priority to cyclists
Have priority over cyclists because it is part of the road
May park on it freely

explanation.short: A suggestion lane is part of the carriageway; you may use it when needed but cyclists keep priority.
explanation.examTip: A solid (separated) cycle path, by contrast, is off-limits to cars.

stem: A lane is marked 'LIJNBUS'. May you drive your car in it?

choices.text: Yes, when traffic in the other lanes is heavy
Yes, but only between 19:00 and 07:00
No, it is reserved for line buses and other authorised vehicles
Yes, if your car has more than two occupants

explanation.short: A lijnbus lane is for line buses, trams and authorised vehicles only.
explanation.examTip: Cross such a lane only at marked breaks.

stem: May you drive your car on tram tracks set in the road?

choices.text: Yes, always — they are part of the road
Only when there is no other safe option and you do not obstruct the tram
Only on Sundays, when fewer trams run
Never under any circumstances

explanation.short: You may drive on tram tracks only when no other safe option exists and you do not obstruct the tram.
explanation.examTip: Trams cannot swerve; leaving them room is a strict duty.

stem: When must you indicate?

choices.text: Only when changing direction at a junction
Only on motorways
Whenever you change direction or lane, or pull out of a parking space
Only if another road user is visible

explanation.short: You must indicate for every change of direction or lane, including pulling out.
explanation.examTip: Cancel the indicator as soon as the manoeuvre is complete.

stem: By indicating to change lane, you:

choices.text: Gain priority over traffic in the target lane
Only announce your intention; you must still yield until safe
Are allowed to move immediately
Make the manoeuvre legal even without checking mirrors

explanation.short: Signalling announces intent; it does not grant priority.
explanation.examTip: Always check mirrors and blind spot before moving.

stem: You are on the acceleration lane joining a motorway. The correct approach is to:

choices.text: Stop at the end of the lane and wait for a clear road
Match the speed of motorway traffic, then merge into a gap
Force your way in; motorway drivers must let you in
Cross all lanes immediately to the left-most lane

explanation.short: Build up to motorway speed on the acceleration lane and merge into a gap.
explanation.examTip: Drivers already on the motorway are not legally obliged to make room for you.

stem: After overtaking on a motorway, you should:

choices.text: Stay in the left lane until the next junction
Return to the right lane as soon as it is safe
Move to the middle lane and stay there
Reduce speed sharply to let others pass on the right

explanation.short: Return to the right lane as soon as it is safe to do so.
explanation.examTip: Using the left lane purely to drive faster is not allowed.

stem: On a quiet three-lane motorway with no other traffic around, which lane should you use?

choices.text: Whichever you prefer
The right-hand lane
The middle lane to stay flexible
The left-hand lane for a smoother ride

explanation.short: You must drive as far right as is practical; the other lanes are for overtaking.
explanation.examTip: Hogging the middle lane is a common scoring point against learners.

stem: On a motorway, when may you pass another car on the right (undertaking)?

choices.text: Whenever the right lane is empty
Never under any circumstances
In slow-moving lanes of dense traffic, where your lane simply moves faster
Only if you flash your headlights first

explanation.short: Overtaking on the right is forbidden, but in dense traffic the right-hand lane may legitimately move faster than the lane to its left.
explanation.examTip: You should not weave between lanes to gain speed — that is overtaking on the right.

stem: One of two lanes ends due to roadworks and traffic is dense. What is the correct technique?

choices.text: Merge as early as possible to free up the ending lane
Use both lanes up to the merge point, then merge one-by-one (zipper)
Force your way in as quickly as possible
Stop and wait for police direction

explanation.short: The zipper method — using both lanes to the very end and taking turns — keeps traffic flowing best.
explanation.examTip: Drivers in the through lane should leave a gap for one merging vehicle each.

stem: You see a lane marked 'BUS' with a solid white line on its left. May you drive a private car in it?

choices.text: Yes, at any time
No, not in a private car
Only at night
Only if there are no buses in sight

explanation.short: A lane marked 'BUS' is reserved for buses (and any vehicles signed as exceptions); private cars must stay out.
explanation.examTip: A separate sign sometimes adds taxis or bicycles — read what is signed.

stem: You are driving in the middle lane of a three-lane motorway. The right lane has been clear for the past kilometre and a lorry is 300 metres behind you in the right lane. What should you do?

choices.text: Stay in the middle lane so you are ready to overtake the next slow vehicle
Move to the right lane now, since it is genuinely free
Move to the left lane to leave space for the lorry
Slow down and let the lorry pass, then stay in the middle lane

explanation.short: On a motorway you must return to the rightmost free lane after overtaking.
explanation.examTip: A test scenario with an empty right lane is almost always a keep-right question.

stem: On a rural road behind a slow tractor you see a broken centre line and 300 metres of clear straight road, but a side road joins from the left 100 metres ahead. Should you overtake?

choices.text: Yes, the centre line allows it and the road ahead looks clear
Yes, but only at well below the speed limit
No, a vehicle could emerge from the side road into your overtaking path
Only if you can complete the overtake before reaching the side road

explanation.short: A junction during an overtake creates the risk of a head-on collision with an emerging vehicle.
explanation.examTip: Treat any junction ahead as a no-overtake situation, even with a broken line.

stem: You want to park on the right of a narrow street. Between you and the kerb is a red cycle suggestion lane (fietsstrook) with broken white lines. What is allowed?

choices.text: Park inside the cycle suggestion lane, since it is part of the carriageway
Cross the suggestion lane to reach the kerb and park on it
Cross the suggestion lane to reach the parking space, but you may not park inside the lane itself
You may not cross the lane at all; you must find another street

explanation.short: You may cross a broken-line cycle suggestion lane, but you may not park inside it because that blocks cyclists.
explanation.examTip: Continuous lines around the red strip would change the answer — then you may not cross at all.

stem: You are on a motorway acceleration lane next to slow-moving traffic on the motorway. Your acceleration lane is running out. What should you do?

choices.text: Come to a full stop at the end of the acceleration lane and wait for a long gap
Keep moving, match the speed of the motorway traffic and merge into a realistic gap
Continue onto the hard shoulder until traffic clears
Signal and force your way in; motorway traffic must yield to you

explanation.short: Match the speed of motorway traffic and merge into a real gap; stopping on the acceleration lane is dangerous.
explanation.examTip: Motorway traffic is not obliged to make room, but a stopped car at the end of the merge lane is the worst outcome.

stem: On a quiet three-lane motorway with no other vehicles around you, which lane should you normally use?

choices.text: The middle lane, for an even view
The left lane, for the highest speed
The right lane — always drive as far right as practical
Any lane you prefer

explanation.short: On Dutch roads you keep right whenever possible; the left and middle lanes are only for overtaking or when traffic dictates.
explanation.examTip: Sitting in the middle lane on an empty motorway is a common scoring point for examiners.

stem: On a 50 km/h road inside a built-up area you want to overtake a cyclist. Oncoming traffic is approaching but a gap is opening. What is the correct lateral distance?

choices.text: Around 30 cm — keep close to save room
At least 1 to 1.5 metres of clear lateral space
As close as possible to avoid the oncoming car
Distance does not matter at 50 km/h

explanation.short: Give cyclists about 1 to 1.5 metres of clearance; if there is not enough room because of oncoming traffic, wait.
explanation.examTip: If the oncoming gap forces you to squeeze past, do not overtake — hold back behind the cyclist.

stem: You want to change lanes on a busy motorway. In what order should you perform the steps?

choices.text: Signal, change lanes, then check mirrors
Check interior and side mirrors, check the blind spot, signal, change lanes when safe
Change lanes first, then signal
Signal only if other cars are close

explanation.short: The CBR sequence is mirrors → blind spot → signal → manoeuvre, never the other way round.
explanation.examTip: Skipping the blind-spot check is one of the most common fails on lane changes.

stem: You see a lane on your right marked with the word 'BUS' and a solid line separating it from your lane. There is no other traffic. May you drive in it?

choices.text: Yes, because there is no other traffic
Yes, but only at low speed
No — a bus lane is reserved for buses and other authorised vehicles only
Yes, but only to overtake

explanation.short: A bus lane is only for buses (and any other vehicles specified on the sign), regardless of how empty it looks.
explanation.examTip: Driving in a bus lane attracts a fine even if there is no bus in sight.

stem: On a quiet 80 km/h provincial road with one lane in each direction and a wide soft shoulder, where should you position your car?

choices.text: Half on the shoulder to give oncoming traffic more space
Centred in your lane, well clear of the verge
As close to the centre line as possible
Crossing the centre line on left-hand bends

explanation.short: Stay centred in your own lane — the shoulder is for emergencies and pedestrians, and edging towards the centre line is dangerous on a narrow rural road.
explanation.examTip: A soft shoulder can pull the car off the road if you put a wheel on it at speed.

stem: On an 80 km/h rural road you have decided to overtake a slow lorry. The broken centre line allows it and the road is straight. Just before you pull out, what is the most important final check?

choices.text: A glance over your left shoulder for a faster vehicle already overtaking you
Your fuel gauge
The right-side mirror only
Whether the lorry has indicated

explanation.short: A blind-spot check over the left shoulder catches a motorcycle or fast car already overtaking you — the most common cause of overtaking collisions.
explanation.examTip: Mirror, signal, blind-spot, manoeuvre: the blind-spot check is the step most often skipped.

stem: Inside a built-up area you signal to turn right into a side street. A red-coloured cycle suggestion lane (broken line) runs along the right side of the carriageway and a cyclist is on it, going straight on. What must you do?

choices.text: Cross the cycle lane and turn — cars have priority over a suggestion lane
Wait for the cyclist to pass, then turn across the cycle lane
Sound the horn and turn at the same time
Drive in the cycle lane up to the turn

explanation.short: Cyclists going straight on have priority over a turning car, regardless of whether the cycle lane is a suggestion or a dedicated path.
explanation.examTip: Most right-hook collisions happen because the driver did not look in the right-hand mirror just before turning. Always check it last.

stem: Traffic is dense and a lane ends 100 metres ahead due to roadworks. You are in the ending lane. What is the correct Dutch technique?

choices.text: Stop in the lane and wait for someone to flash you in
Drive to the very end of the lane, indicate, and zip-merge with the other lane
Merge as early as possible to be polite
Use the hard shoulder to bypass the queue

explanation.short: The Dutch 'ritsen' rule asks drivers to use the full length of the ending lane and merge alternately at the end — this keeps traffic moving smoothly.
explanation.examTip: Merging early in dense traffic is widely seen as unhelpful — it wastes the available road and slows everyone behind.

stem: On a motorway, traffic in the left lane is moving at walking pace while your right lane is moving at 60 km/h. May you stay at 60 km/h and pass cars on your left?

choices.text: No — passing on the right is never allowed
Yes — when traffic on the left has formed a slow-moving queue, you may keep your normal lane and pass on the right
Only at night
Yes, but only if you flash your headlights first

explanation.short: Dutch law permits passing on the right when the left-hand lane is queued. You stay in your lane at your normal speed.
explanation.examTip: This is not the same as undertaking. You must keep your lane — weaving in and out to overtake on the right remains forbidden.

stem: You are behind a slow tractor on a rural road. There is a solid white centre line. The road ahead appears clear for 300 metres. May you overtake?

choices.text: Yes — clear visibility of 300 metres makes it safe to cross a solid line
No — a solid centre line means no overtaking, regardless of visibility
Yes, if you complete the overtake before the next bend
Yes, but only if you use your hazard lights while overtaking

explanation.short: A solid white centre line is a no-overtaking marking. You may not cross it to overtake, no matter how good the visibility looks.
explanation.examTip: Solid lines are placed where sight distance or conditions make crossing dangerous — trust the marking, not your own visual estimate.

stem: You are driving a private car and the bus lane beside you is completely empty. Traffic in your lane is queued. May you use the bus lane to bypass the queue?

choices.text: Yes — an empty bus lane may be used by any vehicle
Yes, but only if no bus is visible behind you
No — a bus lane is reserved for buses, taxis, and emergency vehicles; private cars are not permitted
Only if you drive below 30 km/h in the bus lane

explanation.short: Bus lanes are reserved for buses and explicitly permitted vehicles (taxis, emergency services, sometimes cyclists). Private cars are not allowed, even when the lane is empty.
explanation.examTip: Check the blue bus-lane sign carefully — it lists all permitted vehicle types. If your vehicle type is not shown, you may not enter.

stem: You are on a two-lane road outside a built-up area. Traffic is light. Which lane must you use when not overtaking?

choices.text: Either lane — both are equally available in light traffic
The left (faster) lane, to stay away from roadside hazards
The right lane — you must keep right when not overtaking
The left lane, so overtaking vehicles can use the right lane

explanation.short: Outside built-up areas on roads with two or more lanes, you must drive in the rightmost lane when not overtaking. This is the keep-right obligation.
explanation.examTip: Left-lane hogging is illegal in the Netherlands and a common CBR topic. Keep right unless actively overtaking.

stem: A faster vehicle behind you begins to overtake you on a two-lane road. What should you do?

choices.text: Accelerate to maintain the gap between you and the overtaking vehicle
Keep your speed and lane — the overtaking driver must manage the whole manoeuvre
Move toward the right side of your lane and slightly reduce speed to help the overtake
Brake sharply to let them pass as quickly as possible

explanation.short: When being overtaken, move as far right as safe and marginally reduce speed to give the overtaking vehicle more space and time to complete the manoeuvre.
explanation.examTip: Never accelerate when being overtaken — it is dangerous and deliberately obstructing an overtake can be an offence.

stem: You are driving on a road that shares space with tram tracks. The tram behind you is catching up and wants to pass. What should you do?

choices.text: Maintain your speed — the tram must wait behind you
Move off the tram tracks as soon as it is safe to do so and let the tram pass
Accelerate to stay ahead of the tram
Use your hazard lights to warn the tram to slow down

explanation.short: Trams have priority on their tracks and cannot steer around obstacles. Move off the tracks as soon as it is safe and let the tram pass.
explanation.examTip: Never race a tram or expect it to brake for you — trams need much longer distances to stop than cars do.

stem: You want to overtake a slow cyclist on a country road but you are approaching the brow of a hill with limited forward visibility. May you overtake?

choices.text: Yes — cyclists are slow so there is enough time to overtake before the top
Yes, if you do it very quickly before reaching the hilltop
No — you must have a clear view ahead before beginning an overtake
Yes, by briefly using the oncoming lane

explanation.short: Overtaking when you cannot see far enough ahead is forbidden. The hilltop creates a blind zone — an oncoming vehicle could appear at any moment.
explanation.examTip: Wait until you are past the hilltop and have a long, clear view ahead before overtaking any vehicle, including cyclists.

stem: Before changing lanes on a motorway, what is the correct sequence of actions?

choices.text: Signal → mirror → blind spot check → move
Mirror → blind spot check → signal → move
Blind spot check → signal → mirror → move
Signal → move → mirror check

explanation.short: The correct sequence is: mirror (check following and adjacent traffic), blind spot (shoulder check), signal (inform others), then move.
explanation.examTip: Signalling before checking your mirrors is a common mistake. Signal only once you are sure the move is safe — not as an announcement that you intend to check.

stem: On a motorway with three lanes, you are cruising in the middle lane. The right lane is empty. What should you do?

choices.text: Stay in the middle lane — it gives you more space on both sides
Move to the right lane — you must use the rightmost available lane when not overtaking
Move to the left lane to make room for overtakers behind you
It is the driver's choice which lane to use on a motorway

explanation.short: The keep-right rule applies on motorways too. When the right lane is clear you must move back to it, even if you plan to overtake again shortly.
explanation.examTip: Sitting in the middle lane of a three-lane motorway when the right is empty is a common and fined offence in the Netherlands.

stem: You are driving at night on an unlit rural road and want to overtake. What extra precaution is required compared to daytime?

choices.text: No extra precautions — headlights provide all the visibility you need
Flash full-beam headlights at the vehicle ahead before starting
Ensure the oncoming lane is visible for a much greater distance, as headlight range is far less than the daylight sight distance needed for an overtake
Only overtake if the other driver waves you past

explanation.short: At night your stopping distance is unchanged but you can see much less far ahead. You need a far greater clear distance before overtaking than your headlights may reveal.
explanation.examTip: Night overtaking is one of the most dangerous manoeuvres. If your headlights reach 100 m but your stopping distance is 75 m, an oncoming car at the edge of your beam leaves almost no margin.

stem: A cycle lane marked with a solid white line runs along the right side of the carriageway. May a car drive in it briefly to reach a parking space?

choices.text: Yes — a cycle lane is not mandatory for cars, so cars may use it
Yes, but only for loading and unloading
No — a solid-line cycle lane may not be entered by motor vehicles
Only if no cyclist is visible in the lane at that moment

explanation.short: A cycle lane bordered by a solid white line is mandatory for cyclists and prohibited for motor vehicles. Only a dashed-line suggestion lane may be crossed.
explanation.examTip: Solid line = mandatory cycle lane, no motor vehicles. Dashed line = suggestion lane, may be crossed with caution.
